topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My stomach clenched, a tight, acidic knot forming just beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, my fingers trembling slightly as I scanned the header. The headline was mine—my carefully crafted analysis of the city council's new zoning proposal—but the byline? It belonged to some unknown individual named 'Marcus Thorne.' A slow, hot flush crept up my neck, turning my skin prickly. I reread the text, searching for a mistake, a typo, anything to rationalize this grotesque deception. There was nothing. Just Thorne’s smug, self-serving prose masquerading as my own work. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet office. I stood up, pacing the small space, my jaw tight enough to crack. I needed to find the editor, needed to confront whoever had pulled this stunt. Every nerve ending felt frayed, buzzing with a furious, trapped energy.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my breath catching in a shallow, ragged gasp. The realization hit me with the force of a physical shove, knocking the air from my lungs. My hands clenched into fists, the knuckles whitening instantly. It was a complete fabrication. My words, my research, my voice—all appropriated by some imposter. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, a sound born of pure, simmering outrage. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under the sudden tension in my frame. I started rapidly clicking through my drafts folder, a frantic, jerky motion. Each file opened was another confirmation of the theft. I snatched my phone, my thumb hovering over the contact for the publication’s legal department, my voice dropping to a low, dangerous register. "This is unacceptable," I managed to hiss, the words tasting like ash.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I didn't move immediately. For a long moment, there was only a profound stillness, a terrifying vacuum where my composure usually resided. Then, the stillness shattered. I let out a sharp, involuntary exhale, a sound full of suppressed fury. My gaze swept across the screen, landing on the unfamiliar name. A wave of burning heat washed over my chest, making my pulse hammer against my collarbone. I gripped the edge of my desk so hard my nails dug crescent moons into the wood. I stood up abruptly, my movements jerky and uncontrolled. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, my knuckles white where I gripped the frame. I felt a profound sense of injustice, a hot, blinding need to tear down whatever structure allowed such a brazen act to occur.
